extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is a lab‑scale gantry crane that emulates a harbor crane from the Port of Antwerp. It consists of a structural frame with a travel rail, a hoist mechanism driven by a stepper motor (Component: Stepper) that is controlled by a TMC4671‑Eval motor controller, position sensors, and an embedded controller interfaced via a Python‑based Physical Crane Interface. The crane moves small pucks that represent containers between predefined locations, providing a lightweight, reproducible cyber‑physical test‑bed for research and teaching in the domain of logistics and automation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides several services: • Trajectory Generator Service – uses a kinematic model of the crane to compute optimal, swing‑free movement trajectories (optimization). • Simulation Service – runs a real‑time simulation of the crane and its controllers, enabling prediction and what‑if analysis. • Continuous Validation Service – continuously compares executed trajectory measurements with simulated ones, computes distance metrics, checks against a threshold, and raises alerts on the Grafana dashboard (monitoring and validation). • Data Logging – a Logging Application continuously records crane state to a TimescaleDB database (monitoring). • Visualization – a Grafana dashboard visualizes logged data, simulation confidence bounds and alerts. • MQTT Broker – mediates signaling among the services. Target users are the operator (who monitors and controls the crane via the Human‑Machine Interface) and researchers (who use the simulation, validation and trajectory services for experiments).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The twin is built with open‑source tooling: Python applications implement the Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Human‑Machine Interface and all twin services; Docker Compose orchestrates the containerised services; TimescaleDB stores time‑series data; Grafana provides the visualization dashboard; an MQTT broker enables inter‑service communication; the kinematic and simulation models are custom Python code; all source code and documentation are publicly hosted on GitHub. The hardware enablers include the TMC4671‑Eval motor controller and a stepper hoist motor.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital twin is orchestrated as a set of six Docker‑compose containers: a TimescaleDB instance storing all logged data, a Grafana dashboard visualising the database, a Trajectory Generator Service that consumes a kinematic model to compute swing‑free trajectories, a Simulation Service that mimics the crane dynamics and its controllers, a Continuous Validation Service that continuously compares the measured trajectory (received from the Logging Application) with the simulated trajectory and raises alerts when a distance metric exceeds a configurable threshold, and an MQTT broker that provides publish/subscribe signalling between the services. The physical side consists of five containers (Physical Crane, Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Human‑Machine Interface) all implemented in Python and communicating with the twin via the MQTT broker and by writing state updates into the TimescaleDB. The whole system can be started with a single `docker compose up` command (Fig. 7), reflecting an ad‑hoc, container‑based orchestration rather than a commercial DT framework.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration is realised through standard open‑source protocols and tools: MQTT is used for event‑driven messaging between the twin services and the physical‑side applications; data persistence and exchange with analytical components is performed via SQL access to the TimescaleDB database; the Grafana dashboard pulls data over HTTP/REST from the same database for visualisation. All components are packaged as Docker containers and can be deployed on any host that supports Docker, enabling straightforward integration with external monitoring or automation platforms that understand MQTT or SQL. No proprietary enterprise systems or external standards (e.g., OPC-UA, AutomationML) are employed in this exemplar.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The digital twin comprises a CAD geometric model of the gantry crane structure and a kinematic analytical model that computes the crane’s motion. The Trajectory Generator Service consumes the kinematic model to produce optimal movement trajectories that are swing‑free at the end point. A Simulation Service hosts a dynamic simulation of the crane and its low‑level controllers, reusing the same kinematic model. All runtime measurements (e.g., motor positions, timestamps) are streamed from the Physical Crane Interface to a TimescaleDB instance, which serves as the central data repository. Historical logs are saved continuously by the Logging Application. MQTT is used as the message‑bus for signalling between services. The Continuous Validation Service reads both the simulated trajectory and the measured trajectory from the database, computes distance metrics, and checks them against a predefined acceptance threshold. The database thus integrates model outputs and sensor‑derived data, enabling bi‑directional data flow between the physical system and its models.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a stepper hoist motor driven through a TMC4671‑Eval motor controller. The controller is accessed by the Physical Crane Interface, a Python program that sends commands to the motor driver over the controller’s native communication interface (e.g., SPI/I²C). The Control Application issues high‑level motion commands (position, homing, zeroing) which are translated by the interface into low‑level drive signals. Actuation range and speed limits are defined by the stepper motor specifications (not detailed in the documents). Safety constraints such as homing and zeroing procedures are handled by the Control Application before motion commands are dispatched.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is addressed by ensuring that the kinematic model can generate trajectories with zero residual swing, providing high‑precision motion planning. Validation is performed continuously by the Continuous Validation Service, which compares the measured executed trajectory (logged by the Logging Application) with the simulated trajectory from the Simulation Service. Distance metrics are calculated and compared against an acceptance threshold; violations trigger operator notifications via the Grafana dashboard. This runtime validation constitutes the verification method for the twin’s models and data, and the use of a central TimescaleDB ensures traceability of both measured and simulated data for quality assurance.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical crane (e.g., swing‑angle encoder, anemometer) is read by an Arduino attached to the crane, accessed through the Physical Crane Interface – a Python program that connects to the crane controllers. The Logging Application continuously logs the crane’s state into a TimescaleDB time‑series database. These logged measurements are then consumed by digital‑twin services (e.g., Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service) via the database. Service coordination uses an MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto) for signalling. The documents do not specify exact data types, sampling frequencies, event triggers, preprocessing steps, or data‑quality assurance mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are produced by the Control Application (a Python application) and by services such as the Trajectory Generator Service, which compute optimal trajectories. These commands are sent to the Physical Crane Interface, which forwards them to the crane’s controllers to drive the motors and electromagnet. The documents do not detail command message formats, validation, authentication, feedback, conflict‑resolution, or execution confirmation mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The digital‑twin software is built from Docker containers: TimescaleDB for the database, Eclipse Mosquitto for the MQTT broker, and Grafana for the dashboard, all orchestrated with docker‑compose on a personal computer. Inter‑service signalling uses MQTT. The deployment runs on a single host, with no explicit mention of bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or additional security protocols beyond the use of MQTT and container isolation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All components (physical‑crane interface, control and logging applications, database, MQTT broker, dashboard, and the various twin services) are hosted on‑premise on a personal computer using Docker containers. Required resources include a PC capable of running Python and Docker, storage for TimescaleDB, and network loopback for MQTT communication. The system is open‑source, can be reproduced on other machines, and scales only within the limits of a single‑host Docker deployment; no cloud platforms, high‑availability, or explicit scalability mechanisms are described.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The exemplar follows a four‑stage DT life‑cycle. In the **modeling stage** CAD models of the gantry crane and a kinematic model are created. In the **service‑implementation stage** Python applications (Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Human‑Machine Interface) and DT services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation) are developed, the data layer (TimescaleDB) and visualisation layer (Grafana) are set up, and an MQTT broker is added for inter‑service signalling. The **deployment stage** packages all components into Docker containers and brings them up with a single Docker‑Compose command, providing a reproducible environment. Finally, the **operational stage** runs the twin continuously: the Logging Application stores sensor data, the Simulation Service reproduces the motion, the Trajectory Generator creates swing‑free trajectories, and the Continuous Validation Service compares measured and simulated trajectories, triggering alerts on the Dashboard. The system can also be run without the DT, indicating an optional activation of the twin during operation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The development uses the C4 modelling approach to define the architecture, and all artefacts are version‑controlled on GitHub for reproducibility. Python is the implementation language for both physical‑crane interfaces and DT services, and Docker‑Compose provides containerisation and orchestration. Quality assurance is achieved through a **Continuous Validation Service** that automatically compares live measurements with simulated trajectories, computes distance metrics and raises threshold‑based alerts, thereby acting as an automated test oracle. Fault‑detection research builds on the same validation pipeline with additional time‑series classification techniques. Evolution of the twin is documented with the **DarTwin notation**, which captures structural and behavioural changes across iterations, and the open‑source nature of the project allows incremental extensions (e.g., adding new services or refining the kinematic model) while keeping the repository up‑to‑date.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides decision support through several analytics services. The **Trajectory Generator Service** computes optimal, swing‑free motion plans using the kinematic model, offering operators pre‑validated trajectories. The **Continuous Validation Service** continuously evaluates executed motions by calculating distance metrics between measured robot paths and simulated trajectories; if the metric exceeds a configurable threshold, an alert is sent to the operator via the Grafana Dashboard. The Dashboard visualises real‑time and historical data (including confidence bounds derived from the Simulation Service) enabling operators to assess performance, detect anomalies, and decide on corrective actions. Confidence measures are expressed as upper and lower bounds on simulated trajectories, and threshold parameters can be tuned by the user through the Human‑Machine Interface.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to

actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

Those traces have also been simulated by the simulator, as indicated by the upper (in blue) and lower (in yellow) confidence bounds derived from the simulation. Fig. 8. Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester.

I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

encapsulate the examples and is built on the open ARCHES

The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and

_A. Architecture_

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give



an overview of the components powering it. We should note
that for this physical system, we take a liberal interpretation
of what goes in a C4 Model component diagram.
Figure 4 shows this component diagram, again for brevity
and readability only the most necessary relationships are
shown. It contains the **Crane Frame** consisting of all the
hardware constituting the physical structure of the crane.
Two **Motors** and associated **Controllers**, one to control the
cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement. An
**Electromagnet** at the end of the hoist to pick up containers,
a **Swing Angle Encoder** measures the swinging motion of
the hoist, and an **Anemometer** measures the wind speed. An
**Arduino** measures and controls those last three components.
Lastly a **PSU** powers the electronics of the crane.


_B. Implementation_


Regarding the implementation, we wanted to keep the
operation of the system as simple as possible, that is, plug
in the physical crane to a personal computer, and run all of
the software on that computer. Figure 5 shows the distribution
of the C4 Containers visually.
For the implementation, we bundled all the Python C4
Containers in a Python module named _gantrylib_ . To use
this module, it suffices to install it with _pip_ . This way it
remains easy for researchers to implement additional digital
twin services.

For ease of deployment, the **Database**, **MQTT Broker** and
**Dashboard** C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. A

docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and
down with a single command. For the first time setup, all of
the database and dashboard initialization are also automatically
handled by this docker compose file.


Furthermore some of the components warrant a small explanation for their implementation choices. For the database,
the choice for TimescaleDB was driven by three factors: (i)
it’s based on PostgreSQL, allowing for commonly known SQL
queries for access. (ii) it’s optimized for time series data, which
is what is logged. (iii) it’s open source. For the dashboard, the
choice for Grafana was made because of (i) it’s wide support
for data sources and (ii) again the fact that it’s open source.
Lastly, for the MQTT Broker, the ubiquitous and open-source
Eclipse Mosquitto was used.

# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.

_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**

**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be

Grafana Dashboard showing trajectory traces. _A. Usage in Publications_ The first publication using the exemplar is [4], a short abstract on the concept of continuous validation. While the paper itself did not yet feature the gantry crane, the accompanying poster used the crane as illustrative example. Later, in [5], we further explored the concept of continuous validation through the reuse of existing model validation techniques. Here the initial version of the crane exemplar was developed and used in the evaluation of the paper. For more detailled results w.r.t. the continuous validation digital twin we refer the reader to this publication. The next use of the crane was in [7], where we explored DarTwin, a notation for digital twin evolution. We identified multiple commonly used digital twin evolution. On the one hand the development process of the crane was used to derive those evolutions, on the other hand we envisioned multiple future evolutions of the crane system to demonstrate the DarTwin notation. The latest use of the exemplar was in [6], where we explored state-of-the-art time series classification techniques to find faults in systems in operation. In essence, we extended the continuous validation method of [5] with classification techniques to better pinpoint system faults in the exemplar. _B. Usage in Teaching_ At the University of Antwerp, we organized a Blended Intensive Programme (BIP) on the topic of Model Based Systems Engineering for Digital Twin Systems. Here the exemplar was used as project to work on. We expanded it to an entire harbor system, with a container identification system to intake containers in the harbor, and a ship digital twin with a buoyancy model to teach about correct container loading order. This BIP was organized during the first semester of academic year 2024-2025. In academic year 2025-2026 it will be revisited in the second semester.

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.

VI. C ONCLUSION


We presented a lab-scale gantry crane exemplar designed
to support digital twin research and education. It expands
the amount of available digital twin exemplars and provides researchers and educators an off-the-shelf use case,
reducing their development effort. To facilitate adoption and
reproducibility, the exemplar is openly available on GitHub
along with supporting documentation. The exemplar’s utility
was illustrated with examples from our personal academic
publications and teaching use cases. While we deem the labscale gantry crane mature enough to be presented as exemplar,
it still has some limitations, which we discussed and for which
we provided pointers for future work.
